dateStatements.label: composition or adaptation
dateStatements.value: late fourteenth or early fifteenth century

entities.description: Anonymous Middle English prose adaptation of part of the Latin Elucidarium, with substantial omissions, rearrangement, and fourteen added question-and-answer units.
entities.label: Lucidarie
notes: Lewis argues that calling the work a Lollard text is misleading: it combines orthodox and potentially heterodox positions and may have been influenced by Lollard ideas without being safely classified as Lollard.
Neither surviving copy identifies a Latin source or acknowledges translation.

rights.basis: The work was composed around the turn of the fifteenth century and its anonymous creator died centuries before modern copyright terms.
rights.component: medieval Middle English text as an abstract work
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, or photography.

dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.value: late eleventh or early twelfth century; possibly as early as 1096

entities.description: Three-book Latin theological dialogue traditionally attributed to Honorius Augustodunensis and widely translated and adapted in medieval Europe.
entities.label: Elucidarium
notes: Honorius is retained as the traditional or attributed Latin author. The physical Latin exemplar used by the Middle English adapter has not been identified.

dateStatements.label: manuscript date
dateStatements.value: first half of the fifteenth century

holding.notes: No stable current item-level public catalog URL was located; the generic library and exact request route are recorded under access.

entities.label: Cambridge, St John's College, MS G.25
notes: Schmitt's base witness and the more careful and more complete of the two copies.
James calls the ending on f. 16r unfinished; the preserved final master response is syntactically complete but lacks a formal explicit.
Twenty-nine and twenty-eight lines per page in the codex description; the first two quires containing the Lucidarie are written by Hand A in textura semi-quadrata.
Sarah James reports that Hand A is localized by LALME to Northamptonshire; this is a witness-scribe dialect localization, not a place of composition.

rights.basis: St John's requires a request for reproduction permission and supplies images in formats appropriate to private research or publication.
rights.component: new reproductions and publication use of St John's G.25
notes: The medieval text itself is public domain; this record is restricted to modern reproduction and permission conditions.

access.accessMethod: Submit the online Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory form for private-study copies, publication images, or permission to use existing CUL-supplied images.
notes: The form separates copies for private study from copies and permission for academic or commercial publication. The stated minimum order charge is GBP 25 before VAT, postage, or other charges; a complete-manuscript order may require extra delivery time and conservation review.

rights.basis: CUL's imaging terms require a signed copyright declaration for private-study copies and prior written permission for reproduction; the dedicated form separates private-study, publication, and permission-only requests. No open image license for this manuscript was located.
rights.component: new images or remote reproductions of CUL Ii.6.26
notes: The medieval text itself is public domain; this record is restricted to modern reproduction and service terms.

access.accessMethod: Arrange consultation with Cambridge University Library Manuscripts staff.

holding.notes: The Apollo item is a transcription of an unpublished M. R. James description, not a complete current catalog or manuscript facsimile.

entities.label: Cambridge, University Library, MS Ii.6.26
notes: The Lucistrye follows twelve tracts favoring vernacular scripture. It is a rougher copy than J and has extensive physical lacunae.

physicalDescription.extent: 103 leaves; the collation separately notes two flyleaves and a pastedown; Lucistrye on ff. 79v-101v
notes: Twenty-five to twenty-six lines per page; clear black handwriting with a slight slope; paper boards.

The codex is physically defective. James's collation records a major gap and final quire losses; Lewis describes as many as seven quires missing between ff. 88 and 89 and a possible further loss between ff. 96 and 97.

rights.basis: CUL's imaging terms require a signed copyright declaration for private-study copies and prior written permission for reproduction; the dedicated form separates private-study, publication, and permission-only requests. No open image license for this manuscript was located.

notes: The medieval text itself is public domain; this record is restricted to modern reproduction and service terms.

next_steps: Collate every Middle English exchange in Schmitt pp. 29-35 against the numbered questions in Lefevre's Latin edition.
Check whether Lewis counts a split, compound, or differently numbered Latin question that Marx's table groups differently.
Use 'a small set of Book II questions' in summary fields until the numbering discrepancy is resolved.
openQuestions.question: Why does Lewis state that the Lucidarie includes six questions from Latin Book II when her following detailed sequence and Marx's Table 2 identify II.8 plus II.3-4, 6-7, a set of five numbered Latin questions?

next_steps: Attempt the Google Books PDF download interactively in a normal browser and preserve the exact provider-delivered file if available.
Ask a HathiTrust member-library user to test the Full View download controls for uc1.b2644788.
Keep the thirty-six-page legacy selection explicitly partial until the complete provider object is cached.
notes: Automated Google downloads redirected to CAPTCHA and HathiTrust browser access returned Cloudflare HTTP 403.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete local research copy of Schmitt's xxix + 35-page edition be obtained from the verified Google Books or HathiTrust public-domain routes without bypassing access controls?

next_steps: Email special.collections@joh.cam.ac.uk with shelfmark G.25 and request the catalog record and complete-facsimile status.
If images are supplied, cache the complete codex or complete work-bearing quires according to repository permission and record exact coverage.
Do not treat Lewis Figure 1, f. 15r, as more than a one-folio reproduction.
openQuestions.question: Does St John's College have a complete digitization or stable item-level catalog record for MS G.25 that is not publicly indexed?

next_steps: Ask mss@lib.cam.ac.uk for the current catalog record, digitization status, and imaging options.
Request a Virtual Reading Room review of ff. 79v-101v and the physical gaps if a full image order is not feasible.
Preserve any complete provider object and document the exact folio-to-image crosswalk.
openQuestions.question: Does Cambridge University Library have a complete digital facsimile or current item-level catalog record for MS Ii.6.26 beyond the public M. R. James description?

next_steps: Inspect f. 16r-16v directly for ruling, quire construction, erased text, catchwords, and evidence of loss.
Compare the ending with U, the Latin source sequence, and other Middle English Elucidarium adaptations.
Distinguish absence of a formal explicit from loss of intended textual matter.
openQuestions.question: Why does James call the G.25 Lucidarie ending 'unfinished' when the preserved final master response on f. 16r is syntactically complete and is treated by Schmitt and Lewis as the more complete copy?

next_steps: Collate Schmitt's U page milestones 158-202 and apparatus notes against images of ff. 79v-101v.
Reconstruct the codex collation around present ff. 88/89 and 96/97 without assuming that later page numbers map continuously across missing quires.
Record separate physical, paginal, and textual lacuna intervals.
openQuestions.question: How do Schmitt's internal-page lacuna statements for U relate exactly to the quire losses and present foliation described by James and Lewis?

next_steps: Collate the Middle English readings and sequence against Lefevre's Latin edition and current Elucidarium stemmatic scholarship.
Separate direct translation, paraphrase, omitted questions, rearranged questions, and the fourteen additions.
Do not identify either surviving Middle English witness or Schmitt's printed Latin alignment as the adapter's physical source.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin Elucidarium recension or physical exemplar underlies the anonymous Middle English adaptation?

relations.subjectLabel: Sarah James, A Previously Unnoticed Extract of Suso's Horologium Sapientiae in English (2012)

reviewNotes: Middle English is the language of the medieval work. German occurs in Schmitt's title page, editorial notes, and omitted introduction and is not transferred to the work-level language statement.
Lucidarie is treated as an anonymous work-level adaptation. Honorius is associated only with the Latin source work, not assigned as author of the Middle English work.
Schmitt's thirty-four chapter headings and two-book division are editorial source alignment, not manuscript divisions.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00140. It encodes the title page and all thirty-five numbered text pages of Friedrich Schmitt's 1909 edition, but not the dissertation's xxix-page German introduction. Schmitt based his text on Cambridge, St John's College, MS G.25, ff. 1r-16r, and collated Cambridge University Library MS Ii.6.26, ff. 79v-101v; the latter is physically defective. The anonymous Middle English Lucidarie, composed or adapted in the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, is kept distinct from the Latin Elucidarium traditionally attributed to Honorius Augustodunensis, the two manuscript witnesses, Schmitt's edition, its complete Google/HathiTrust scan, and the repository's legacy thirty-six-page selection PDF. A complete public-domain edition viewer was verified, but automated whole-volume retrieval was blocked by CAPTCHA or Cloudflare; neither manuscript has a complete public facsimile located as of review. Exact reading-room and reproduction routes are therefore recorded alongside a published partial image of St John's G.25, f. 15r.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; first attested in two early-fifteenth-century witnesses; first verified publication in 1909
catalogSummary.region: England; no place of composition is established, and the Northamptonshire localization of the first St John's scribe is retained only as witness-level dialect evidence
catalogSummary.title: Lucidarie

catalogingSubject.label: Lucidarie
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Middle English prose adaptation of part of the Latin Elucidarium, preserved in two early-fifteenth-century Cambridge manuscripts as Lucidarie and Lucistrye. The work omits Latin Book III, selectively adapts much of Book I and a small set of Book II questions, and adds fourteen question-and-answer units. CME00140 represents Friedrich Schmitt's complete thirty-five-page edited text and apparatus plus his title page, but omits the edition's xxix-page German introduction. Medieval work, Latin source, manuscript witnesses, modern edition, digital encoding, and repository artifact remain distinct descriptive layers.

contentParts.label: Lucidarie
notes: J and U likely descend from a common exemplar; neither is identified as the other's source.
J is more careful and more complete, but James's description of its ending as unfinished remains unresolved.

dateStatements.display: 1909 (first verified publication of this two-witness version)

scope.description: First scholarly publication located for the Lucidarie/Lucistrye version.

extent.description: One prose work. No intrinsic medieval section count is established because neither witness has book or chapter divisions. For representation analysis, CME00140 contains two DIV2 book wrappers with thirty-four DIV3 chapters (31 + 3), thirty-five HEAD elements, 291 P elements, thirty-six PB elements, 494 NOTE elements, seventy-four manuscript MILESTONE elements (thirty J folio markers and forty-four U page markers), and 847 HI elements. Those are Schmitt/XML editorial metrics, not an item or medieval-structure count.

formStatements.description: Continuous prose dialogue between disciple and master.

scope.description: The complete Middle English work.
Master-disciple question-and-answer form addressing Christian doctrine.
Instructional dialogue intended to clarify doctrine and support lay religious understanding.
Selective translation, paraphrase, rearrangement, and original or externally derived additions relative to the Latin Elucidarium.
Vernacular prose on theology, devotion, ecclesiology, and moral instruction.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German in CME00140 belongs to Schmitt's 1909 title and apparatus, not to the medieval work; Latin source passages and citations are source or quotation layers.

scope.description: The language of the medieval work in both surviving witnesses.

notes: Lucidarie is preferred because it is the J title and common scholarly title; Lucistrye is retained as U's equally real witness title.
The long German title in CME00140 is Schmitt's edition title, not an original or preferred title of the medieval work.

Honorius Augustodunensis is the traditional or attributed author of the Latin Elucidarium only. U-M and Hathi catalog headings that place Honorius in the author position are not transferred to target-work responsibility.
The work is one prose dialogue. Schmitt's two books and thirty-four chapters are modern source alignment, and the content parts record them only as analytical representation segments.
German and Latin material in CME00140 is editorial, source, or quotation-layer content; the work-level language statement remains Middle English.

The Lollard label is deliberately excluded from positive classification and tags because Lewis finds it misleading. This does not erase reformist content or foreclose more precise future doctrinal analysis.
J and U likely share a common exemplar. J is Schmitt's base and more complete; U is a collateral defective witness, not a derivative of J.
CME00140 encodes the complete thirty-five-page Middle English text selection of Schmitt's edition but not the complete printed dissertation, whose German introduction occupies pp. i-xxix.

next_steps: Collate Schmitt pp. 29-35 against Lefevre's numbered Latin questions.
Determine whether a split, compound, or alternate numbering convention explains the discrepancy.
Retain qualitative wording in catalog summaries until the count is reconciled.
openQuestions.question: Why does Lewis state that the Lucidarie includes six questions from Latin Book II when her detailed sequence and Marx's Table 2 identify II.8 plus II.3-4, 6-7, a set of five numbered Latin questions?

next_steps: Collate distinctive readings and question order against current Latin Elucidarium editions and stemmatic scholarship.
Search for shared lexical and doctrinal features in other late Middle English religious dialogues without converting witness dialect into composition place.
Retain anonymous translator-adapter responsibility and England-only cultural origin until direct evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Who produced the Lucidarie, where was it composed, and which Latin Elucidarium recension or physical exemplar did the adapter use?

next_steps: Inspect f. 16r-16v, the end of the second quire, and any catchword or erasure evidence.
Compare J's terminal response with U, the Latin source sequence, and other Middle English Elucidarium versions.
Do not call J absolutely complete merely because it is more complete than U.
openQuestions.question: Is the J text physically or textually incomplete at f. 16r, or did James use 'ends unfinished' only for the absence of a formal explicit?

next_steps: Obtain images of ff. 79v-101v and the adjacent quire boundaries from Cambridge University Library.
Crosswalk U pp. 158-202, present foliation, Schmitt's apparatus gaps after pp. 176 and 192, and Lewis's quire reconstruction.
Record physical lacunae separately from material omitted by the Middle English adapter.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact textual and physical extents of U's lacunae, and how do Schmitt's later page-number references map to the quire losses described by James and Lewis?

next_steps: Compare the added exchanges with securely dated reformist and Wycliffite theological texts at the level of individual doctrines and phrasing.
Study the distinct compilation contexts of J and U as evidence of reception rather than proof of composition origin.
Keep Lollardy out of positive genre, subject, and tag fields unless a future controlled term explicitly represents scholarly debate rather than categorical identity.
openQuestions.question: How should the Lucidarie's mixture of reformist, orthodox, and potentially heterodox positions be situated more precisely without restoring the misleading categorical label 'Lollard text'?

assertion.notes: This does not convert the Northamptonshire dialect localization of J's Hand A into a composition-place assertion.

scope.description: Selective vernacular rendering of Latin Elucidarium material.

notes: The work omits Latin Book III, uses only a small set of Book II questions, restructures Book I, and adds fourteen question-and-answer units; Lewis's summary count of six Book II questions and the five numbered questions in her detailed sequence and Marx's table require reconciliation.

scope.description: Omission, rearrangement, paraphrase, and addition beyond direct translation.
Modern editorial title page, transcription, apparatus, chapter alignment, and typographic intervention represented in CME00140.
Overarching subject of the master-disciple instruction.
God, Trinity, creation, and the created order in the opening adaptation.
Angelology, demonology, creation and fall of humanity, and consequences of sin.
Incarnation, life and passion of Christ, redemption, and ascension.
Sacramental presence, reception, priestly status, and corrupt clergy.

assertion.notes: The presence of reformist criticism does not by itself license a categorical Lollard genre or subject label.

scope.description: Criticism of clerical wealth and vice, secular correction of corrupt priests, religious orders, and ideals of Christian poverty.
Church corruption, precursors of Antichrist, tribulation, and the end of the world.
The source-derived Book II questions and the two added final exchanges on grace.
Intercession and the final response's image of Mary, Christ, martyrs, and saints as tokens of divine grace.
The abstract Middle English work.

summaries.value: The Lucidarie is an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a disciple questions a master about Christian doctrine. It selectively adapts much of Book I and a small portion of Book II of the Latin Elucidarium, omits Book III, and inserts fourteen additional exchanges. Subjects range from the Trinity, creation, angels, the Fall, incarnation, redemption, Eucharist, and priesthood to clerical corruption, religious poverty, Antichrist, sin, free will, grace, Mary, and the saints. Although earlier scholarship identified the work as Lollard, Anna Lewis argues that its mixture of orthodox and potentially heterodox positions makes that label misleading; possible influence is not converted into a categorical work classification.

scope.description: High-level intellectual contents without treating Schmitt's imposed chapter headings as medieval structure.

summaries.value: The opening follows the Latin Book I dialogue selectively through God and the Trinity, creation, angels and demons, humanity and the Fall, redemption, Christ's life and passion, ascension, Eucharist, priesthood, and corrupt clergy. Near the end, twelve added question-and-answer units are interleaved with a Book II question on social or ecclesiastical orders. The final segment adapts Book II questions on evil, sin, and free will, then ends with two further added exchanges on whether every willing petitioner may receive grace. The manuscripts themselves do not divide the work into books or chapters; the two representation segments below summarize source alignment established by Schmitt, Lewis, and Marx.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary between work and representation layers.

summaries.value: CME00140's XML language attribute lists Middle English and German because the encoding preserves Schmitt's German title page, apparatus, and supplied notes. Work-level language is Middle English only. The XML's two books, thirty-four chapters, 494 notes, and J/U milestones describe the 1909 editorial representation; neither witness carries the book and chapter divisions. Schmitt's title Die mittelenglische Version des Elucidariums des Honorius Augustodunensis is therefore an edition title, not the preferred title of the anonymous medieval work.

assertion.notes: The St John's copy bears this title, and Lewis says it is the title most often used because J is the more careful and more complete witness.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

notes: Preserved in U's opening rubric and as a variant in Schmitt's apparatus.

scope.description: Alternate witness title supplied by CUL Ii.6.26.

notes: This title describes source relationship and language; it is not evidence that the Middle English text names Honorius or acknowledges translation.

scope.description: Descriptive scholarly title for the vernacular adaptation.
The opening rubric of J as printed by Schmitt and encoded in CME00140.

work.summary: The Lucidarie is an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue in which a disciple questions a master about Christian doctrine. It selectively adapts much of Book I and a small portion of Book II of the Latin Elucidarium, omits Book III, and inserts fourteen additional exchanges. Subjects range from the Trinity, creation, angels, the Fall, incarnation, redemption, Eucharist, and priesthood to clerical corruption, religious poverty, Antichrist, sin, free will, grace, Mary, and the saints. Although earlier scholarship identified the work as Lollard, Anna Lewis argues that its mixture of orthodox and potentially heterodox positions makes that label misleading; possible influence is not converted into a categorical work classification.
